This is not exactly to your point, but years ago, when my nephew was about 8, we were all watching a Bruce Lee movie. My nephew started a conversation that went roughly like this:

*Nephew:*  When I grow up, I want to be like him. *Points to Bruce Lee*

*His mom:*  Well, you can be anything you want. If you work hard and do your best, you can be anything. Would you like to take a karate class?

*Nephew:*  No 

*Me (summoning some uncle energy):*  To learn karate you'll need to practice. It takes work.

*Nephew:*  I don't want to know karate.

*His mom:*  You just said you wanted to be like him when you grow up.

*Nephew:*  Yeah.  I want to be like him. I want to be Chinese!

The punchline here is obviously that he was a white kid. But the point is....kids are not going to let something like skin color stand in the way of their aspirations and imaginations.

replies.author: @mikoto7693
replies.text:
@Jaskol_WazonYou’re not alone, I promise you that. Skin colour never mattered to me although people have made it harder for me to explain it properly without being thick or obtuse over the years.

I gave (and still give it) no more importance or weight than eye colour, or hair colour and type, or being short or tall. It’s just one of many personal features that everyone has that distinguishes themselves as a unique individual.

I’ve always found culture and nationality makes people more different than me than skin colour. Generally speaking, a person with different skin colour that has been raised in the same culture and country as me will be far more similar to me than another person with the same skin colour but raised halfway around the planet with an entirely different culture and values could ever be.

Like, seriously, skin colour means nothing beyond an adaptation to the conditions they developed in. Cold, hot, humid or dry. None are better or worse than any of the other races. Just a little different but equal.

I don’t really get what the fuss is about. It’s long past the time that people stopped being so dumb and shallow and obsessed with cruelty of the distant past. Nobody has been a legal slave or slaver today during our lifetimes. Stop living in the past obsessing about ancient crimes and focus on forging a better future for everyone.

replies.author: @davidgantenbein9362
replies.text: It’s about a standard starting budget for an MCU movie (usually around 200 million). But we have to consider that this amount wouldn’t include any reshoots nor the marketing. As there is a whole villain (Sidewinder) that was hired for reshoots, we can expect the reshoots to be massive (probably another 50% budget) and marketing for a blockbuster would be another 100+ million. So total expenses are probably somewhere north of 300 million, maybe around 400 million. That gives us a break even point of roughly 800 million global box office. Not too unreasonable a business decision if this was a good MCU movie, as good MCU movies also make a lot of money with merchandise, tv etc. after the cinema, so that a 2 times target return is achievable.

replies.author: @LRich-hg3hu
@LRich-hg3hu
replies.text:
​​​isheanmmo9357I thought that would have worked better in the series.

1. Sam refused the shield, because though he's a good man, he doesn't feel he's truly a hero (on Cap's level).

2. Bucky takes the shield, thinking this will be his shortcut to redemption.

3. Public backlash against Bucky as Cap because of his past.

4. That sends Bucky into a pretty dark place. And Sam is there to help him even though others say he shouldn't. 

5. In taking on that role of "stalwart and true friend" Sam (and the audience) learns he has a lot more of a "true Captain America" inside him than other movies let us see before.

6. Sam (and hopefully viewers) is able to see himself as worthy of being Cap. He's his own man, his own hero. But he has the same basic values and convictions Steve did. He doesn't need to be a perfect man, he needs to be a good man. And an aspect of being a good man (and Cap) is stepping up to do what's right even when that seems like a burden, a sacrifice, and even when you don't think you're the one for the job.

7. Series ends with Sam taking up the shield.

That's not a storyline that would blow anyone away. But I think it would have had people more eager to see this movie.

replies.author: @LRich-hg3hu
replies.text:
isheanmmo9357 The same plot using new characters doesn't get the same result. (Particularly, in this instance, because Walker has no need to seek redemption by becoming Cap.)

If a studio could insert any new character into any plot, the MCU wouldn't make sequels at all. They'd invent new characters for each new plot. If that worked, they'd do it. Because that alone would cut production costs by tens of millions per film.

Story = Character (People) + Plot (Events).

Same people with different events yields a different story.

Different people with the same events also yields a different story.

replies.author: @iwasakikoji6969
replies.text: @LRich-hg3hu That's literally what happened in the comics, though. Bucky became Cap as part of a redemption arc. There's an actual story to be told there. When Sam became Cap in the comics, it was just sort of his turn. While it made perfect sense in the lore, it had little to no storytelling potential and was very lackluster (not even mentioning that, unlike Bucky, when Steve Rogers came back, Sam didn't stop being Captain America because you're not allowed to demote a black guy).

replies.author: @LRich-hg3hu
replies.text:
isheanmmo9357 That isn't the same though. As you are describing it:

Bucky would become Cap to seek redemption. Whereas, Walker would need redemption because he became Cap. Bucky wants to become Cap because he had murdered people ...Walker murdered people because he became Cap. Bucky wants the shield to prove he is "good" ...Walker took up the shield and proved he is not "good." 

Bucky's redemption would begin by taking up the shield. Walker's redemption would start by putting it down.

Those are VERY different plots (and both could be good stories). As well as 2 different characters, and Walker being one that audiences have no investment in, unlike Bucky. And to my original issue on this....if Walker and Bucky seek redemption and it plays out the same way (as you say it would)....then Walker and Bucky are narratively the same person just wearing different skin. There's no personality differences because they react precisely the same to events.

And if Bucky and Walker are the same person narratively, then one of them needs to not be in the show. It's should be Captain America and the Winter Soldier, or Captain America and John Walker. Not Captain America and the Winter Soldier, but also John Walker.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
The obvious option is they never read the book. But 'Brave New World' is in reference to The Tempest. I think the Miranda character states "Oh, wonder! How many goodly creatures are there here! How beauteous mankind is! O brave new world, that has such people in 't!". As a reference to Huxley, it's confusing. As a reference to Shakespeare (and assuming Shakespeare didn't mean it ironically), you'd need to decode whether the idea is that making Mackie Captain America is an indication of a beautiful world (which seems like you'd need to be kinda enamored with the character), or that it's just an expression of optimism - given that even the somewhat villainous president character finds some modicum of redemption (via the moral guidance of the minority lead). What a world where the villainous old white man isn't shot, stabbed and immolated! 

I don't see how 'new world order' applied unless they had MASSIVE rewrites. So maybe they just were going with whatever sounded cool.

someone wrote a smart review or tweet (can't find it right now) that Marvel sold out storytelling to get asses in seats. They revealed Red Hulk in the trailers for how many months now? They gave away the end of the movie, trading suspense and surprise for Hulk hype. Audiences don't usually have patience for characters trying to solve a mystery they have already solved. 

Similar problems come from casting Ford - which was a big mistake. He's been phoning it in for years, and he wasn't trying to fill in for Hurt playing Ross at all. He was being franchise Ford, but that name gets the fans in seats. If they cared about storytelling, they'd have got a seasoned theater or character actor who could mimic Hurt enough to pull off a decent closure for Ross. Ford just takes you out of the movie. He doesn't tie into the history of Ross over 5 movies and 12 years. There was no continuity of character. 

Sebastian Stan in prosthetics and makeup could have more successfully played Ross for this movie. Stan would have completely captured the speech pattern and mannerism, and not been a diva about the motion capture labor.

text: To me, the biggest issue is that Anthony Mackie is not a great actor. He’s not bad, but he’s also not particularly impressive. I don’t understand how he keeps landing these roles—whether it’s Captain America, his character in Twisted Metal, or his latest film, Elevation. He simply doesn’t have the presence or charisma that makes you think, That’s a guy I’d want to be like one day or That’s someone who dominates on screen. In my opinion, he’s better suited for supporting roles rather than leading the franchise.
